prompt: How do SOTA bottoms-up B2B security SaaS package and meter SELF-SERVE billing in 2026? kolm has: free Scan, $750 one-time Signed Report, $299/$999 mo Continuous, $25k named-co-signer Attestation, all flat, Stripe. Research: free-tier limits that convert without cannibalizing, usage/seat/agent metering norms, in-app upgrade UX, card-swipe vs invoice thresholds, annual discounts, the proof-of-value moment placement, and where a self-serve ceiling should hand off to sales. Cite real pricing/packaging (with URLs). Recommend the billing/packaging refinements (NOT price changes - prices are locked) that maximize self-serve conversion: metering, limits enforcement, upgrade prompts, dunning, receipts/invoices.
content:
**SOTA bottom-up B2B security/devsec SaaS (e.g., Snyk, Semgrep) in 2026 typically combine freemium with usage- or contributor-based metering, clear free-tier limits for proof-of-value (PoV) without heavy cannibalization, in-app upgrade flows, Stripe-powered self-serve (card for lower tiers), and a handoff to sales/enterprise at scale or complexity thresholds.**[[1]](https://snyk.io/plans/)[[2]](https://semgrep.dev/pricing)[[3]](https://konvu.com/compare/snyk-vs-semgrep)

Kolm’s current flat structure (free Scan; $750 one-time Signed Report; $299/$999/mo Continuous; $25k named Attestation via Stripe) is a solid foundation for a security scanning/attestation product but can be refined for better self-serve conversion through metering/limits, UX prompts, and billing ops—without altering listed prices.

### Key SOTA Practices from Security SaaS Examples
- **Free-tier limits for conversion without cannibalization**: Snyk offers a Free plan ($0, unlimited contributing developers) with hard monthly test/scan limits (e.g., ~200 SCA, 100 SAST, 100 Container, 300 IaC tests/mo depending on product). This enables quick PoV via scans while pushing paid upgrades for volume. Semgrep’s Community/Free tier supports up to ~10 contributors/repos with core features but routes heavier use to paid. These limits are generous enough for individuals/small teams to experience value (scans/reports) but restrictive for production/continuous use.[[1]](https://snyk.io/plans/)[[4]](https://checkthat.ai/brands/snyk/pricing)[[3]](https://konvu.com/compare/snyk-vs-semgrep)

- **Metering norms (usage/seat/agent)**: Per-contributor/developer is dominant in devsec tools (Snyk Team ~$25/dev/mo; Semgrep ~$30/contributor/mo per module like Code or Supply Chain). Usage metering appears via scan/test volume, repos, or workloads. Hybrid models (base subscription + overages/credits) are rising, especially for variable security workloads. Seat/agent metering fits “Continuous” monitoring; scan volume or attestation requests suit one-time/usage elements.[[2]](https://semgrep.dev/pricing)[[5]](https://corgea.com/blog/compare/snyk-vs-semgrep)

- **In-app upgrade UX and PoV placement**: Upgrades are prompted in-app when limits are approached (e.g., “You’ve used 80% of monthly scans—upgrade for unlimited”). PoV moment is typically the first successful scan + actionable report (free tier) or signed/continuous output. Self-serve portals (Stripe Customer Portal or equivalent) let users upgrade/downgrade, view usage, and manage payments without leaving the app.[[6]](https://stripe.com/resources/more/saas-pricing-and-packaging-strategy)

- **Card-swipe vs. invoice thresholds and annual discounts**: Self-serve card payments dominate below ~$5k–10k ACV or low monthly tiers; invoices or “Contact Sales” kick in for enterprise/custom (e.g., SSO, compliance, high volume, or named attestation at scale). Annual plans are standard with discounts (commonly 10–20%, sometimes positioned aggressively higher like 20–35% effective to drive commitment, though benchmarks vary).[[7]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/brandonsriggs_heres-another-hard-truth-about-saas-pricing-activity-7331423926482296834-SXtl)[[8]](https://www.wingback.com/blog/should-you-offer-discounts-for-yearly-subscribers)

- **Self-serve ceiling and sales handoff**: Self-serve typically caps at ~10–50 contributors/users or moderate usage (e.g., Semgrep Free to 10 contributors; Snyk Team capped ~10 devs before higher tiers). Beyond that—or for features like advanced compliance, unlimited volume, custom integrations, or high-value attestations—route to “Contact Sales” or enterprise plans. This preserves PLG velocity at lower ACVs while capturing larger deals.[[9]](https://chartmogul.com/reports/saas-go-to-market-report/)[[10]](https://deepsource.com/resources/snyk-alternatives)

Real examples (as of mid-2026 data):
- Snyk: https://snyk.io/plans/ (Free with test limits; Team $25/dev/mo; higher tiers per-dev or custom).[[1]](https://snyk.io/plans/)
- Semgrep: https://semgrep.dev/pricing (Free/Community up to 10 contributors; Team $30/contributor/mo per product; Contact Sales for custom/enterprise).[[2]](https://semgrep.dev/pricing)

Broader B2B SaaS patterns emphasize usage-based/hybrid metering (API calls, scans, seats, data processed) with Stripe for self-serve billing, customer portals for upgrades/invoices/receipts, and automated dunning.[[11]](https://stripe.com/resources/more/usage-based-pricing-strategy-for-saas)[[12]](https://stripe.com/resources/more/self-serve-subscription-management-and-billing-portals)

### Recommended Refinements for Kolm (Maximize Self-Serve Conversion)
Focus on **metering/limits enforcement, upgrade prompts, dunning, and receipts/invoices** (prices unchanged). These leverage Stripe’s capabilities for a seamless, low-friction experience:

- **Metering & limits enforcement (free tier + paid)**: Introduce soft/hard limits on the free Scan tier (e.g., X scans or repos per month, or Y scans per repo) to mirror Snyk/Semgrep—enough for PoV (quick security insights) but not sustained production use. For Continuous tiers, meter by agents/seats scanned or total scan volume (enforce via entitlements); one-time Signed Report and Attestation stay flat but could gate on verified usage history. Use real-time metering to block excess or prompt upgrades. This prevents free-tier cannibalization while converting high-value users.[[13]](https://www.stigg.io/blog-posts/usage-based-pricing)

- **In-app upgrade UX and PoV placement**: Surface contextual prompts (e.g., dashboard banners or modals at 70–80% of free limits: “Unlock continuous monitoring—upgrade now”). Place the primary PoV moment immediately after the first scan (free report preview) or signed output. Embed upgrade CTAs directly in the app with one-click Stripe Checkout for card-swipe tiers. Add a usage dashboard showing “scans used this month” and projected costs.

- **Card-swipe vs. invoice thresholds**: Keep low-tier Continuous ($299/$999) and one-time items fully self-serve via Stripe card. Route $25k Attestation or high-volume/enterprise requests (e.g., >N agents or custom co-signer needs) to an invoice flow or “Contact Sales” form. Use Stripe Invoicing for the latter.

- **Annual discounts & packaging**: Package monthly vs. annual options on the pricing page (same headline prices, but annual billed upfront with an effective discount communicated clearly—e.g., “Save X% with annual”). This drives commitment without price changes.

- **Dunning, receipts & invoices**: Enable Stripe’s automated dunning (smart retries, failed-payment emails, account updater) to minimize involuntary churn. Provide self-serve access to downloadable receipts/PDF invoices and billing history via the Stripe Customer Portal (or embedded equivalent). Support prorated upgrades/downgrades and clear cancellation flows.

- **Self-serve ceiling & sales handoff**: Define explicit thresholds (e.g., >10–20 agents, >certain monthly scans, or Attestation requests) that trigger in-app “Talk to Sales” or PQL routing. Keep pure self-serve viable up to mid-market while smoothly escalating complex/high-value deals.[[14]](https://www.revenuehero.io/blog/plg-without-sales-assist)

These changes align with 2026 norms, improve conversion by making value clear and upgrades frictionless, reduce support load via self-serve tools, and maintain Stripe as the backbone. Track metrics like self-serve upgrade rate, free-to-paid conversion, and time-to-PoV to iterate.
